did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning (specifically 18 CNNs including VGG16, ResNet, and InceptionResNetV2) to evaluate the effect of Dark Corner Artifact (DCA) removal on binary classification of melanoma vs. non-melanoma skin lesions. It also employs automated DCA detection/removal methods and Grad-CAM for analyzing network activations in the context of skin lesion classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including the curation of a new dataset of 9,810 images with new artifact labels, the development of automated DCA detection and removal methods, and the evaluation of these methods using image quality metrics (SSIM) and binary classification tasks on skin lesions. It reports specific results and contributions rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Dark Corner on Skin Lesion Image Dataset: Does it matter?
journal: 2022 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshops (CVPRW)
brief_summary: This study investigates the impact of Dark Corner Artifact (DCA) on binary classification of melanoma versus non-melanoma skin lesions using a curated balanced dataset of 9,810 ISIC images. The authors introduced new artifact labels and developed automated methods for DCA detection and removal to improve image quality. Performance was evaluated using Structural Similarity Index (SSIM) on the Dermofit dataset, where the proposed method achieved better scores across all DCA intensity levels. Additionally, the study analyzed the effect of DCA removal on deep learning models, specifically 18 CNNs, by examining network activations via Grad-CAM. The results demonstrate that while classification accuracy differences were marginal, removing DCA helps shift network activations toward the actual skin lesions rather than the artifact.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task (melanoma vs non-melanoma) using a curated balanced dataset of 9,810 ISIC images.
The comparator class in the binary classification task, representing non-melanoma skin lesions from the ISIC dataset.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (melanoma vs non-melanoma skin lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper evaluates the effect of Dark Corner Artifact (DCA) removal on a binary classification task distinguishing between melanoma and non-melanoma skin lesions using 18 CNNs (e.g., VGG16, ResNet, InceptionResNetV2). The study also involves automated DCA detection (binary classification of artifact presence) and uses Grad-CAM to analyze network activations for class prediction.

datasets_used.name: Curated Balanced ISIC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A curated balanced dataset of skin lesion images proposed by Cassidy et al. [5], consisting of an equal number of melanoma and non-melanoma images. It was thoroughly checked for duplicate images to reduce bias. The dataset contains 9,810 images total.

use.training: Used for training deep learning models (18 CNNs) and as the primary dataset for artifact annotation and analysis.
use.validation: Used for validation of deep learning models and artifact distribution analysis.
use.other: Used for automated Dark Corner Artifact (DCA) detection and removal method development and evaluation of network activations via Grad-CAM.

datasets_used.name: Dermofit Image Library
datasets_used.description: An unseen dataset used for evaluating the performance of the proposed DCA removal methods using image quality metrics.

use.testing: Used for testing image quality metrics (SSIM) to evaluate the effectiveness of DCA removal methods across different DCA intensity levels.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared model performances using metrics such as accuracy, TPR, TNR, F1, AUC, SSIM, MSE, MAE, and PSNR, noting that differences were 'marginal' but did not perform statistical significance testing.

Dermofit_Image_Library.description: Unseen dataset used for evaluating image quality metrics (SSIM, MSE, MAE, PSNR) of DCA removal methods.

metrics.SSIM: Higher scores achieved by proposed methods compared to baseline across all DCA intensity levels.
metrics.MSE: Lower values achieved by proposed methods compared to baseline.
metrics.MAE: Lower values achieved by proposed methods compared to baseline.
metrics.PSNR: Higher values achieved by proposed methods compared to baseline.

Dermofit_Image_Library.notes: The paper states that both DCA removal methods (Navier Stokes and Telea) produced comparable results, with the greatest improvement seen in images with large DCA. Both methods showed a decrease in MAE performance for images containing small DCA.

Curated_Balanced_ISIC_Dataset.description: Primary dataset (9,810 images) used for training 18 CNNs and evaluating binary classification (melanoma vs non-melanoma) with and without DCA removal.

results.accuracy_difference: Marginal differences in performance between baseline and DCA removal methods.
results.best_performing_model: InceptionResNetV2 (identified as the most common network with the best performance across result sets).

Baseline_Classification.notes: Full quantitative results (accuracy, TPR, TNR, F1, AUC) are not explicitly listed in the text; the paper notes that full results are available at a GitHub repository. The paper highlights that while accuracy differences were marginal, Grad-CAM analysis showed baseline models focused on DCA regions, whereas DCA removal shifted focus to the lesion.

results.activation_shift: Network activations shifted from DCA regions to skin lesions, eliminating bias toward melanoma classification.

DCA_Removal_Classification.notes: Quantitative classification metrics for specific models post-removal are not detailed in the text, only the qualitative shift in Grad-CAM heatmaps and marginal accuracy differences are reported.

Curated_Balanced_ISIC_Dataset.notes: The paper did not report specific accuracy, F1, or AUC values for the classification task in the text, stating only that differences were 'marginal' and directing readers to the code repository for full results.

architecture_used.main_model: 18 CNNs from the Keras Applications zoo: VGG16, VGG19, Xception, ResNet50, ResNet101, ResNet152, ResNet50V2, ResNet101V2, ResNet152V2, InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2, DenseNet121, DenseNet169, DenseNet201, EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB1, EfficientNetB3, EfficientNetB4.
architecture_used.pretraining: No pre-trained weights were used (trained from scratch).
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported in the text.

architecture_details.best_performing_model: InceptionResNetV2 (identified as the most common network with the best performance across result sets).

training_details.hardware: AMD Ryzen 7 3700X 8-core 16-thread 4.4GHz CPU with 16GB DDR4 3000MHz Dual-Channel RAM and an NVIDIA Geforce RTX 3090 FE 24GB GDDR6X GPU.
training_details.software: Python 3.9.7, TensorFlow GPU 2.9.0-dev20220203, CUDA 11.2.1, cuDNN 8.1, Windows 10.

classification_or_output_head.analysis_method: Grad-CAM (Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping) was used to analyze network activations, specifically extracting gradients from the final convolutional layer.

preprocessing_techniques: Dark Corner Artifact (DCA) detection and removal
Intensity reduction of DCA borders (calculating border thickness using Ldist, Rdist, Tdist, Bdist)
Cropping of image borders to remove DCA
Super-resolution using a pre-trained EDSR (Enhanced Deep Residual Networks) model to upscale cropped images
Resizing images back to original dimensions (224x224) after super-resolution
Image inpainting using Navier Stokes method
Masking of DCA regions using minimum enclosing circle contours
Image shuffling and splitting into subsets based on DCA intensity levels

data_augmentation_techniques.details: DCA masks of varying intensities (Small: 7% area, Medium: 25% area, Large: 50% area) were superimposed onto images in the Dermofit dataset to simulate artifacts for evaluation purposes.
Image borders containing DCA were cropped to remove the artifact intensity.
Cropped images were upscaled 4x using a pre-trained EDSR (Enhanced Deep Residual Networks) model to restore image quality, then resized back to original dimensions (224x224).
Used to predict and fill the remaining DCA region after intensity reduction, utilizing fluid dynamics equations adapted for image inpainting.
Used to predict and fill the remaining DCA region after intensity reduction, iteratively estimating points from the edges of the area.
The curated ISIC dataset was balanced with an equal number of melanoma (4,905) and non-melanoma (4,905) images.

conclusions: The study demonstrates that Dark Corner Artifact (DCA) has the potential to skew Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) classifications by introducing a bias toward melanoma predictions, as evidenced by Grad-CAM heatmaps showing network focus on the artifact rather than the lesion.

Automated DCA removal methods (Navier Stokes and Telea inpainting) significantly improve image quality metrics (SSIM, PSNR) on an unseen dataset (Dermofit) across all DCA intensity levels, with the greatest improvements observed in images with large DCA.

While quantitative classification accuracy differences between baseline and DCA-removed datasets were marginal, qualitative analysis via Grad-CAM confirms that DCA removal successfully shifts network activations from the artifact regions to the actual skin lesions.
The proposed DCA removal techniques effectively eliminate the class bias induced by DCA, allowing deep learning models to focus on relevant lesion features rather than confounding artifacts.

The authors acknowledge limitations in the current inpainting methods, noting that while effective for most images, they are not perfect, and suggest future work exploring GAN-based outpainting to further improve removal accuracy and reduce blur.
important_remarks: The study relies on a curated balanced ISIC dataset (9,810 images) which, while large, was collected from patients at increased melanoma risk and predominantly fair-skinned patients in Germany, potentially limiting generalizability to other demographics.

Quantitative classification performance differences between baseline and DCA-removed models were reported as 'marginal' without specific accuracy values or statistical significance testing (p-values) in the text, directing readers to an external repository for full results.

The primary evidence for DCA bias is qualitative (Grad-CAM heatmaps showing shifted activations) rather than quantitative improvement in diagnostic metrics, which may overstate the clinical impact of DCA removal on final classification accuracy.

The automated DCA removal methods (Navier Stokes and Telea inpainting) are not perfect; the authors note that the process can fail on certain images and introduce blur, suggesting limitations in current inpainting techniques for this specific artifact.

The study acknowledges that previous related works often used very small datasets (e.g., 233 or 900 images), highlighting that this paper's larger dataset size is a strength, but also noting that access to large dermatoscopic datasets remains generally limited.

The artifact labels are subjective annotations made under 600x magnification, and the authors note that different annotators might yield different results, introducing potential inter-annotator variability.
